q3: The model kept defaulting to organization-as-hero framing — 'we provided,' 'we were able to' — until I explicitly called it out. It also initially separated numbers from stories rather than letting data flow as confirmation of what the story already showed.
q5: The rubric did capture most of what I care about, especially the agency-centered framing and the narrative through-line criteria — those matched my instincts well. But it took my feedback to actually get the model to apply them; having the rubric visible didn't seem to change the first draft much, which tells me the rubric was better at describing good writing than at generating it.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me notice that the model was fairly consistent at the structural level — it knew the right architecture — but kept slipping on the sentence-level execution, particularly subject choice. The rubric named 'agency-centered framing' as priority one, but the model's first instinct was still to write 'we' sentences and then fix them after feedback, which suggests the rubric was informing revision more than generation.

q3: The ending kept defaulting back to the organization as subject — 'We're trying to' type constructions — which I had to flag more than once. It wasn't a catastrophic miss, but it was a recurring pull toward org-centered framing that I had to correct rather than the model catching itself.
q5: I did look at it, and honestly it maps pretty closely to what I actually care about — the agency framing, the donor-as-strategist register, the narrative through-line. What it doesn't quite capture is the sentence-level thing about who is the grammatical subject, which is more granular than 'agency-centered framing' suggests. The rubric got the spirit right but not the craft mechanics.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me notice that the model was consistently strong on structure and narrative arc — it really does build pieces that flow — but the sentence-level subject choices were where it kept slipping. The rubric names agency framing as priority one, but the model seemed to treat it as a paragraph-level concern rather than a sentence-by-sentence discipline, which is where the actual work is.

q3: The model kept stalling on the cost-per-participant number — it refused to estimate for two full rounds before finally giving me a working figure. That was genuinely frustrating because I needed something to plug in, not a perfect audited number, and the back-and-forth cost us time and momentum in the draft.
q5: I did look at it, and honestly it captured a lot of what I care about in language I recognized — the agency framing, the donor as strategic co-architect, the scene-level specificity. What it couldn't capture is the sentence-level instinct I have about who the grammatical subject is, which is the thing I end up correcting most often. The rubric got the philosophy right but the model still needed my feedback to apply it at the word level.
q6: The rubric made me notice that the model was actually quite consistent on the donor paragraph — it protected that framing across all three draft rounds without backsliding, which I wouldn't have consciously credited it for. What it also surfaced is that the model's weak point isn't values or structure, it's nerve — it hedges on specifics and waits for me to give permission to commit to a number or a detail, when I'd rather it made a defensible choice and let me correct it.

q3: The closing ask kept slipping away from the story — even when the tiers had specific dollar amounts, the connection back to Dora or Marisol would dissolve into generic program language. I had to keep pulling it back to the anchoring person, which felt like the same correction across multiple rounds.
q5: The rubric captured most of what I care about, especially the agency-centered framing and the narrative through-line criteria — those are genuinely the things I think about most. What it didn't fully capture is the grammatical specificity I care about: it's not just that community members should be 'subjects,' it's that the sentence-level construction matters, 'Dora enrolled' versus 'we enrolled Dora.' The rubric gestured at that but didn't make it concrete enough to force the model to catch it consistently.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me realize the model was actually doing the scene-level specificity criterion fairly well by the later iterations — the kitchen table detail, the hand-drawn map, those landed. What I wouldn't have noticed without the rubric framing it explicitly is that the model's weakest point was consistently the closing ask, which is where the narrative through-line criterion is hardest to execute and where the model kept defaulting to a pivot rather than a continuation of the story's grammar.

q3: The closing ask kept drifting toward generic — the model understood the story-first structure and the donor framing well, but it took multiple rounds to get the ask to echo Dora's specific language and connect forward to her neighbors or the next cohort rather than pivoting to abstract program outcomes.
q5: The rubric captured a lot of what I care about, especially the agency-centered framing and the scene-level specificity criteria — those felt close to how I actually think about the work. What it didn't fully capture is the sentence-subject thing, the idea that 'Maria enrolled' versus 'we enrolled Maria' is a dignity choice, not just a style choice. The rubric gestured at it but treated it more like a craft preference than a values commitment.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me notice that the model was actually doing the agency framing pretty consistently by iteration 6 — Dora was the subject of her own sentences most of the time — but it was still defaulting to bridge sentences to connect the story to the ask rather than letting the grammar do that work. The rubric named the echo pattern explicitly, which helped, but the model kept wanting to point at the connection rather than embody it.
